chemicals.concentration_notes: The overall toxicity of Milkweed ranges from moderate to severe depending on the amount the cat ingests4. Plants containing cardiac glycosides are considered to be among the most toxic plants and have a very "narrow margin of safety"1. All parts of the milkweed plant, including its sap, are considered toxic
chemicals.description: in cats: These toxins directly target the cardiovascular system by inhibiting the Na + /K + -ATPase pump3. This inhibition causes an accumulation of intracellular sodium and extracellular potassium, which subsequently triggers a rise in intracellular calcium3. This cellular disruption forces the cardiac muscle to contract more forcefully but at a slower rate, resulting in severe abnormal heart rat
chemicals.name: Cardiac glycosides

description: Milkweed (Asclepias spp.) is a toxic plant for cats due to cardenolide cardiac glycosides — potent compounds found throughout the plant, with the highest concentration in the milky latex sap. These compounds act on the heart in a manner similar to Foxglove, disrupting the electrical signals that regulate heartbeat. All parts including dried plant material remain toxic.
name: Milkweed
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: cardenolide cardiac glycosides in latex sap, leaves, and stems
Mechanism: cardenolides inhibit the Na+/K+-ATPase pump in cardiac muscle cells, causing arrhythmias
Symptoms: drooling, vomiting, weakness, abnormal heart rate, muscle tremors; severe cases may cause seizures and cardiac failure
The milky latex sap is the most concentrated source — avoid contact with broken stems
Seek veterinary care promptly; cardiac glycoside toxicity can escalate rapidly

symptoms.name: Vomiting and diarrhea
symptoms.notes: These are generally the first visible signs of cardiac glycoside toxicosis before systemic cardiovascular symptoms become apparent12. 2. Cardiovascular Abnormalities

symptoms.name: Abnormal heart rates and rhythms (including bradycardia, tachycardia, and severe arrhythmias)
symptoms.notes: This is the most dangerous effect of the plant's cardiac glycosides, which disrupt the Na+/K+ ATPase pump and alter how the heart muscle contracts. This can also trigger hyperkalemia (dangerously high potassium levels in the blood), which is life-threatening12. 3. Generalized Weakness and Neurologic

symptoms.name: Weakness and unspecified neurological signs
symptoms.notes: These symptoms manifest as the cardiovascular system becomes profoundly depressed and cellular oxygen/energy cycles are disrupted12. 4. Breathing Difficulties

symptoms.name: Breathing difficulties (dyspnea)
symptoms.notes: Respiratory distress frequently occurs secondarily as the heart begins to fail and blood pressure drops2. 5. Organ Damage
symptoms.onset: Within 2 hours (or developing as the condition worsens).

symptoms.name: Organ damage
symptoms.notes: Depending heavily on the amount of milkweed or toxic sap ingested, systemic toxicity can ultimately cause severe damage to the liver or kidneys
symptoms.onset: Delayed (typically develops as symptoms persist over 4–5 days).

treatments.description: If the cat is currently asymptomatic, the veterinarian will induce vomiting (emesis) to quickly remove the plant material from the stomach. Following this, they will administer a dose of activated charcoal with a cathartic to bind to and prevent the absorption of any remaining toxins
treatments.name: Emesis Induction and Activated Charcoal
treatments.notes: Because cardiac glycosides undergo "enterohepatic recirculation" (recirculating continuously through the liver and intestines), it is recommended to repeat the administration of activated charcoal (without a cathartic) every 6 to 8 hours for a total of 3 doses2. 2. Antidote Administration

treatments.description: The veterinarian administers this specific antidote. While primarily designed for digoxin toxicity, these antibody fragments cross-react and work effectively against plant-based cardiac glycosides
treatments.name: Digoxin-specific antibody fragments (Fab)
treatments.notes: If this specialized antidote is unavailable at the clinic, the veterinarian will have to rely heavily on the pharmacological cardiac controls listed below2. 3. Pharmacological Cardiac Control

treatments.description: The vet will administer specific medications to correct the heart's electrical abnormalities. Atropine and glycopyrrolate have been used successfully to correct severe bradycardia (slow heart rate), while antiarrhythmics like lidocaine are used to manage other heart rhythm defects
treatments.name: Antiarrhythmic and Heart Rate Medications (Atropine, Glycopyrrolate, Lidocaine)
treatments.notes: This treatment is primarily utilized to manage cardiovascular symptoms when access to the Digoxin-specific antidote is not available2. 4. Fluid Therapy and Electrolyte Management

treatments.description: The cat is given IV fluids to provide necessary hemodynamic support, while the vet closely monitors their electrolyte levels to watch for hyperkalemia (dangerously high potassium in the blood)
treatments.name: Intravenous (IV) Fluid Therapy and Hyperkalemia Management
treatments.notes: Mild hyperkalemia can often be treated with IV fluids alone. However, if the hyperkalemia becomes severe (measuring greater than 7.5–9 mEq/L), the vet will need to treat it aggressively using sodium bicarbonate or a dextrose and insulin treatment
